title: The Shanty
content:
آ آ آ آ There are tracks through the scrub, thereï؟½s a track down the hill,
آ آ آ آ And a track round the bend from Mï؟½Courteneyï؟½s mill,
آ آ آ آ Where they slyly emerge from the bush and converge,
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½ll discover the humpyï؟½the theme of this dirge,
آ آ آ آ That is used for the sale of Oï؟½Sullivanï؟½s ï؟½purge.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And if curses and cries,
آ آ آ آ And a blasting of eyes,
آ آ آ آ And a series of blasphemies fearful arise,
آ آ آ آ And a lunatic din,
آ آ آ آ And a racket like sin,
آ آ آ آ You can bet all you own the Oï؟½Sullivanï؟½s in.

آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s a bark and slab hut, with a bar and a bunk,
آ آ آ آ And a man propped before it disgustingly drunk,
آ آ آ آ And a nameless galoot in a hand-me-down suit,
آ آ آ آ Straddling out on the grass, grim as death, and as mute,
آ آ آ آ Trapping millions of rabbits that run from his boot.
آ آ آ آ When eleven lie round
آ آ آ آ In all shapes on the mound,
آ آ آ آ And two navvies are fighting like fiends on the ground,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tisnï؟½t needful to say
آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s the sweet Sabbath day,
آ آ آ آ And that trade at the shantyï؟½s uncommonly gay.

آ آ آ آ Mrs. Oï؟½. makes the drinks, and Oï؟½Sullivanï؟½s dart
آ آ آ آ Is to drink all he can to keep others in heart.
آ آ آ آ Though heï؟½s old in the hoof, and he reckons heï؟½s proof
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Gainst infernalest liquors, in warp and in woof,
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s quite frequently seen howling out on the roof.
آ آ آ آ For from fungus or fruits,
آ آ آ آ From old rags or from roots,
آ آ آ آ Grass, cabbages, pickles, old bedding or boots,
آ آ آ آ Or the leaves of the gum,
آ آ آ آ Or whatever may come,
آ آ آ آ Mrs. Oï؟½. can extract the most illigantï؟½ rum.

آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve no peace in the hut and no peace on the hill,
آ آ آ آ Mrs. Oï؟½. never sleeps and her handï؟½s never still;
آ آ آ آ And old constable Mack cannot hit on the track
آ آ آ آ As a man of the law. As a stranger in black
آ آ آ آ When he finds his way there he canï؟½t find his way back.
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s no signboard to see,
آ آ آ آ But those fools on the spree,
آ آ آ آ Or a man in his shirt shrieking prayers to a tree.
آ آ آ آ As for licensesï؟½yar!
آ آ آ آ They donï؟½t know what they are,
آ آ آ آ For they drink without license at Sullivanï؟½s bar.
meaning:
About: Edward George Dyson (1865-1931) was an Australian poet, journalist and short story writer.

He was born at Morrisons near Ballarat in March 1865. His father, George Dyson, arrived in Australia in 1852 and after working on various diggings became a mining engineer, his mother came from a life of refinement in England. The family led a roving life during Dyson's childhood, moving successively to Alfredton, Bendigo, Ballarat and Alfredton again. Unconsciously the boy was storing for future use the life of the miners, farmers and bushmen, among whom he lived. At 12 he began to work as an assistant to a travelling draper, after that was a whimboy in a mine, and for two or three years an assistant in a factory at Melbourne. This was followed by work in a newspaper office. At 19 he began writing verse, and a few years later embarked on a life of free-lance journalism which lasted until his death. His first notable work was "The Golden Shanty", which appeared in the Bulletin, and many other short stories followed. In 1896 he published a volume of poems, Rhymes from the Mines, and in 1898 the first collection of his short stories, Below and On Top. In 1901 his first long story The Gold-stealers was published in London, which was followed by In the Roaring Fifties in 1906. In the same year appeared Fact'ry 'Ands, a series of more or less connected sketches dealing with factory life in Melbourne in a vein of humour. Various other stories and collections of stories were published in the Bookstall Series and will be found listed in Miller's Australian Literature. Another volume of verse Hello, Soldier! appeared in 1919. All through the years Dyson did an enormous amount of work until he broke down under the strain and died after a long illness on 22 August 1931. He married Miss Jackson who survived him with one daughter.

Edward Dyson was the brother of Will Dyson and Ambrose Dyson.
